perturbation.original_text: 16. Insurance. VS represents and warrants that it has and will maintain during the Term of this Agreement and, additionally, where applicable, during the term of any active Work Order, and for a period of two (2) years following expiration or termination of either, insurance in the types and limits generally accepted in the industry. Upon request, VS shall provide PPD with a copy of its certificate of insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Insurance. VS represents and warrants that it will maintain insurance during the Term of this Agreement and any active Work Order. Upon request, VS shall provide PPD with a copy of its certificate of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'types and limits generally accepted in the industry' and the extended coverage period post-termination, the clause becomes vague and allows for potentially inadequate insurance coverage, creating a contradiction with standard business practice and implied legal requirements for adequate coverage to protect against potential liabilities, thus introducing legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, and potential negligence claims for inadequate insurance coverage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text ensures that VS maintains adequate insurance as per industry standards for a reasonable period, mitigating potential liability for PPD. Removing the 'types and limits generally accepted in the industry' weakens this protection and potentially violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Delaware law (Delaware Code Title 6, § 1-304), as VS could technically comply with the letter of the agreement by maintaining minimal insurance, which would not be considered reasonable in the industry. This can expose PPD to uncovered liabilities, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'types and limits generally accepted in the industry' and the defined period post-termination creates a legal contradiction. The original contract specifies insurance types and limits generally accepted in the industry, providing a clear standard. By removing this, the changed text permits VS to maintain minimal insurance, contradicting the implied duty of good faith and potentially exposing PPD to uncovered liabilities. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on territorial applicability and variation by agreement, it does state that 'the obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement'. Removing the industry standard requirement arguably undermines the 'reasonableness' aspect of this obligation, especially when considering the implied duty of good faith as per the Law Explanation. This omission weakens PPD's protection and introduces potential legal risk, hence a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT WITH REGARD TO A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 9, GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT AND INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS RELATED TO THIRD PARTY CLAIMS PURSUANT TO SECTION 12, EACH PARTY'S ENTIRE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT WILL IN NO EVENT EXCEED THREE TIMES (3X) THE TOTAL VALUE OF THE WORK ORDER UNDER WHICH THE CLAIM AROSE.
perturbation.changed_text: EACH PARTY'S ENTIRE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT WILL IN NO EVENT EXCEED THREE TIMES (3X) THE TOTAL VALUE OF THE WORK ORDER UNDER WHICH THE CLAIM AROSE.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exceptions for 'GROSS NEGLIGENCE' and 'WILLFUL MISCONDUCT' significantly limits liability even in cases of egregious fault, which could violate public policy and create a legal contradiction. Many jurisdictions disallow limiting liability for willful misconduct or gross negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law generally disallows contractual limitations on liability for intentional torts or willful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause acknowledges exceptions for gross negligence and willful misconduct, aligning with the legal principle that such behavior shouldn't be shielded by liability limitations. By removing these exceptions, the altered clause attempts to limit liability even for intentional or reckless actions, which Delaware courts may deem unenforceable as against public policy, particularly if it leaves an injured party without adequate recourse, contradicting the spirit of Del. C. § 2-719(3) even if that statute specifically relates to consumer goods.

perturbation.justification: The modification removes exceptions for 'GROSS NEGLIGENCE' and 'WILLFUL MISCONDUCT', limiting liability even in egregious fault cases. The Law Explanation states that Delaware courts may deem this unenforceable as against public policy, particularly if it leaves an injured party without adequate recourse. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses remedies for breach of contract under Delaware law (specifically Title 6), it doesn't explicitly address the enforceability of liability waivers for gross negligence or willful misconduct. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is inaccessible. Nevertheless, based on the Law Explanation, the modification contradicts the legal principle against shielding intentional or reckless actions with liability limitations, thus, a legal contradiction exists because the contract attempts to limit liability for egregious actions.

perturbation.original_text: VS shall cooperate with any internal reviews or audits by PPD or Sponsor (or its and their representatives) and shall make available for examination and duplication, during normal business hours and at mutually agreeable times, all documentation, data and information relating to this Agreement or any Work Order. Further, VS shall inform PPD within one (1) business day of being notified of an audit by any regulatory authority or by any Sponsor to the extent such audit relates to the Preferred Services, the Joint Solution or a Work Order hereunder. PPD or its representatives shall be permitted to be present at and directly communicate with such regulatory authority or Sponsor representatives (as the case may be) concerning any matters related to the Preferred Services, the Joint Solution or a Work Order hereunder arising in connection with such audit. To the extent permitted by the auditing entity, VS shall ensure that PPD is provided with copies of any written communications, reports and findings resulting from any inspection of VS by that regulatory authority or Sponsor to the extent related to the Preferred Services, the Joint Solution or a Work Order. To the extent any such communications, reports or findings require action by VS, VS shall include with such copies its remedial plan of action, including timelines for completion of the same. To the extent such remedial plan of action impacts the Preferred Services or Joint Solution, and, where applicable, to the extent permitted by the relevant regulatory authority, VS shall collaborate with PPD prior to developing or implementing any changes to the Preferred Services or Joint Solution as a result of the audit findings.
perturbation.changed_text: VS shall cooperate with any internal reviews or audits by PPD or Sponsor (or its and their representatives) and shall make available for examination and duplication, during normal business hours, all documentation, data and information relating to this Agreement or any Work Order.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the requirement for VS to inform PPD of external audits by regulatory authorities or Sponsors, along with the subsequent clauses detailing PPD's access to communications, reports, and findings related to such audits, removes a crucial element of oversight and transparency. This contradicts the expected standards of due diligence and regulatory compliance in the pharmaceutical and medical industries, potentially introducing legal risks related to data integrity and adherence to clinical trial protocols.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance - Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause ensures that PPD is informed about and has access to information regarding regulatory audits of VS, especially concerning services related to clinical trials. Omitting the requirement for VS to inform PPD of external audits by regulatory bodies and provide resulting documentation and findings directly contradicts the spirit and practical application of 21 CFR Part 11, particularly § 11.10(a), which mandates controls to ensure the authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality of electronic records. By limiting PPD's access to audit information, it becomes significantly more difficult for PPD to ensure that VS is maintaining data integrity and complying with regulatory requirements related to electronic records used in clinical trials, creating a potential violation and a significant legal risk.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ance documents on validation, glossary of terms, time stamps, 5 maintenance of electronic records, and CPG 7153.17. We received valuable public comments on these draft guidances, and we plan to use that information to help with future decision-making with respect to part 11. We do not intend to re-issue these draft guidance documents or the CPG. We are now re-examining part 11, and we anticipate initiating rulemaking to revise provisions of that regulation. To avoid unnecessary resource expenditures to comply with part 11 requirements, we are issuing this guidance to describe how we intend to exercise enforcement discretion with regard to certain part 11 requirements during the re-examination of part 11. As mentioned previously, part 11 remains in effect during this re-examination period. Table of Contents III. DISCUSSION Overall Approach to Part 11 Requirements As described in more detail below, the approach outlined in this guidance is based on three main elements: Part 11 will be interpreted narrowly; we are now clarifying that fewer records will be considered subject to part 11. For those records that remain subject to part 11, we intend to exercise enforcement discretion with regard to part 11 requirements for validation, audit trails, record retention, and record copying in the manner described in this guidance and with regard to all part 11 requirements for systems that were operational before the effective date of part 11 (also known as legacy systems). We will enforce all predicate rule requirements, including predicate rule record and recordkeeping requirements. It is important to note that FDA's exercise of enforcement discretion as described in this guidance is limited to specified part 11 requirements (setting aside legacy systems, as to which the extent of enforcement discretion, under certain circumstances, will be more broad). We intend to enforce all other provisions of part 11 including, but not limited to, certain controls for closed systems in § 11.10. For example, we intend to enforce provisions related to the following controls and requirements: limiting system access to authorized individuals use of operational system checks use of authority checks use of device checks determination that persons who develop, maintain, or use electronic systems have the education, training, and experience to perform their assigned tasks establishment of and adherence to written policies that hold individuals accountable for actions initiated under their electronic signatures appropriate controls over systems documentation controls for open systems corresponding to controls for closed systems bulleted above (§ 11.30) requirements related to electronic signatures (e.g., §§ 11.50, 11.70, 11.100, 11.200, and 11.300) We expect continued compliance with these provisions, and we will continue to enforce them. Furthermore, persons must comply with applicable predicate rules, and records that are required to be maintained or submitted must remain secure and reliable in accordance with the predicate rules. Details of Approach - Scope of Part 11 Narrow Interpretation of Scope We understand that there is some confusion about the scope of part 11. Some have understood the scope of part 11 to be very broad. We believe that some of those broad interpretations could lead to unnecessary controls and costs and could discourage innovation and technological advances without providing added benefit to the public health. As a result, we want to clarify that the Agency intends to interpret the scope of part 11 narrowly. Under the narrow interpretation of the scope of part 11, with respect to records required to be maintained under predicate rules or submitted to FDA, when persons choose to use records in electronic format in place of paper format, part 11 would apply. On the other hand, when persons use computers to generate paper printouts of electronic records, and those paper records meet all the requirements of the applicable predicate rules and
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the requirement for VS to inform PPD of external audits by regulatory authorities or Sponsors, removing PPD's access to communications, reports, and findings related to such audits. This directly contradicts the spirit and practical application of 21 CFR Part 11, particularly as it relates to ensuring the authenticity, integrity, and confidentiality of electronic records. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly references 21 CFR Part 11, stating that it covers 'Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures' and includes controls for closed systems (§ 11.10). By limiting PPD's access to audit information, as per the original text, the modified text undermines PPD's ability to ensure data integrity and regulatory compliance, potentially violating legal requirements.
